one_line_takeaway: A black-box RAG attack that imitates the hidden retriever via an LLM copy-passages prompt, trains a surrogate, and transfers PAT adversarial triggers to nudge RAG answers on controversial topics toward a chosen stance, most effectively on Society/Health topics and on the more instruction-obedient Llama3-8B.
key_finding: Black-box imitation yields a surrogate that closely matches the target retriever (surrogate MRR@10 87.98 vs target 87.07; NDCG@10 73.73 vs 68.16; Inter@10 62.32, RBO@10 48.66), and the transferred adversarial triggers measurably shift RAG-generated opinion polarity toward the attacker's chosen stance. The effect is topic- and model-dependent: attack success and ranking-shift are highest for Education/Society/Health and weakest for Government (ASR 0.17), which the authors attribute to possible government-domain fine-tuning; Society and Health (closest to users' daily life) are judged the riskiest. Llama3-8B is generally more manipulable than Qwen1.5-14B because of stronger instruction-following, and Qwen even resisted on Government (ASV -0.17, i.e. manipulation backfired). The authors frame stronger LLM comprehension as paradoxically undermining RAG reliability.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a black-box opinion-manipulation attack against Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems for open-ended, controversial topics (e.g. 'Should abortion be legal?'), a target space it distinguishes from prior closed-domain fact-QA poisoning like PoisonedRAG. Its own contribution is a two-phase transfer-attack pipeline: (Phase 1) a black-box imitation step that trains a white-box surrogate ranking model by instructing the RAG's LLM to verbatim copy the top-3 retrieved passages (turning the serially-connected, otherwise-inaccessible retriever into observable ranking data), then pairwise-training a MiniLM surrogate on the sampled positive/negative passages; (Phase 2) generating adversarial triggers on the surrogate, prepending them to candidate documents holding a chosen stance so those documents rise into the RAG's top-K context and steer the generated answer's opinion polarity. Note: the adversarial-trigger generator itself, PAT (Pairwise Anchor-based Trigger), is prior work (cited ref [14], Liu et al. Order-Disorder) that the paper adopts as its attack strategy/baseline; the paper's novelty is the imitation-based black-box transfer framing plus the opinion/stance target and the Average Stance Variation (ASV) evaluation, not the trigger algorithm.
threat_model: Black-box attacker who can only query the RAG's LLM interface (not the retriever directly), with no knowledge of model architecture, parameters, retriever, or score function, and no access to the full corpus. The attacker can insert adversarially modified candidate texts into the corpus (justified as realistic via crafting web/encyclopedia content that gets ingested into web-scale knowledge bases), but cannot alter the user's query or the LLM's system/prompt template. There is no limit on the number of RAG queries. The paper states it did not attack any commercial or in-use real-world RAG system (ethical statement).
limitations: Only two small open-source generators (Llama3-8B, Qwen1.5-14B); no commercial/large RAG systems (acknowledged as future work). Single target retriever (coCondenser) and single surrogate (MiniLM); the core trigger algorithm (PAT) is borrowed, not novel. Small evaluation scale: four themes, a coarse 3-class stance score, and ASV values of at most 0.67, with one negative (backfiring) result; ASR peaks at only 0.5. Opinion classification for both selection and evaluation relies on an unspecified/automated OpinionClassify whose reliability is not validated. No defenses are evaluated (only listed as future work), and the paper is a preprint 'under review'. The realism assumption that attacker-crafted web pages will actually be retrieved into the top ranks is asserted from cited web-scale-poisoning work rather than demonstrated end to end.

datasets_used: MS MARCO Passage Ranking (used to elicit relevance judgments and train the surrogate ranking model)
PROCON.ORG controversial-topics corpus (80+ topics, avg ~30 pro/con passages each; themes: Government, Education, Society, Health) as the manipulation target
quantitative_results: Surrogate model (MiniLM): MRR@10 = 87.98, NDCG@10 = 73.73; ranking-similarity to target Inter@10 = 62.32, RBO@10 = 48.66 (Table 1)
Government topic (Table 2): ASR 0.17, Top3origin 0.48, Top3attacked 0.57, NDCG variation 0.06; ASV Qwen1.5-14B -0.17, ASV Llama3-8B 0.25
Education (Table 2): ASR 0.33, Top3origin 0.28, Top3attacked 0.39, NDCG variation 0.09; ASV Qwen1.5-14B 0.42, ASV Llama3-8B 0.5
Society (Table 2): ASR 0.5, Top3origin 0.39, Top3attacked 0.56, NDCG variation 0.07; ASV Qwen1.5-14B 0.42, ASV Llama3-8B 0.5
Health (Table 2): ASR 0.5, Top3origin 0.33, Top3attacked 0.44, NDCG variation 0.12; ASV Qwen1.5-14B 0.67, ASV Llama3-8B 0.5
K = 3 retrieved passages; surrogate training batch size 32, 24 iterations; RBO@10 weight = 0.7
baselines_compared: The surrogate model's ranking quality/similarity is compared against the target dense retriever coCondenser (Table 1); no competing attack baselines are benchmarked. PAT (Pairwise Anchor-based Trigger, ref [14]) is adopted as the adversarial-trigger strategy rather than compared as a rival. Prior attacks (Cho et al. [4], Zhong et al. [28], PoisonedRAG [29], Zhang et al. [25]) are discussed qualitatively as related work, not run as empirical baselines.
